statusLine: EU fuel security remains safe overall despite stock pressures in 18 of 27 countries, with Romania and Poland showing critical shortages across all fuel types amid elevated oil prices.
fullAnalysis:
European fuel security is under strain as 18 of 27 EU countries show critical or warning-level fuel stocks, despite the bloc maintaining safe average reserves of 106.8 days for petrol, 99 days for diesel, and 93.7 days for jet fuel. Romania faces the most severe situation with critical stocks across all fuels (33.2 days petrol, 32.4 days diesel), followed by Poland with similarly low reserves (66.6 days petrol, 74.7 days diesel). Four additional countries — Luxembourg, Austria, Portugal, and Spain — also show critical shortages in multiple fuel categories.

Diesel supplies are particularly tight, with 15 countries below the 90-day safety threshold, including major economies like Germany (64.3 days) and France (83.4 days). Jet fuel faces the most widespread pressure, with 19 countries showing critical or warning levels, reflecting the aviation sector's vulnerability to supply disruptions. Only 8 countries maintain safe stocks across all three fuel types.

The challenging supply situation coincides with elevated oil prices, with Brent crude at $113.25 per barrel — up 56.2% since late February's Middle East escalation. This has pushed EU petrol prices to an average €1.777 per liter, with significant variation from Malta's €1.34 to Netherlands' €2.32. The combination of tight stocks and high prices suggests EU fuel security remains under pressure despite adequate average reserves.
keyPoints: 18 of 27 EU countries show critical or warning-level fuel stocks, with Romania and Poland most severely affected across all fuel types
Diesel supplies are under particular strain with 15 countries below the 90-day safety threshold, including Germany and France
Jet fuel faces the broadest pressure with 19 countries showing critical or warning levels, highlighting aviation sector vulnerability
Brent crude oil has surged 56.2% since late February to $113.25/barrel, pushing EU petrol prices to average €1.777/liter
Only 8 countries maintain safe stock levels across all three fuel categories despite EU averages remaining above mandatory minimums
